vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only changes a kernel sysctl parameter and ensures persistence by editing files under /etc/sysctl.d. This does not affect authentication, networking, or boot-critical components and is a standard STIG hardening step. Applying the change is unlikely to lock out access or cause a system failure.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect sysctl syntax could prevent the parameter from being applied; If the file is misedited, the setting may be omitted

# sysctl settings are defined through files in
# /usr/lib/sysctl.d/, /run/sysctl.d/, and /etc/sysctl.d/.
#
# Vendors settings live in /usr/lib/sysctl.d/.
# To override a whole file, create a new file with the same in
# /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there. To override
# only specific settings, add a file with a lexically later
# name in /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there.
#
# For more information, see sysctl.conf(5) and sysctl.d(5).

llm_verdict.message:
The kernel parameter **kernel.kptr_restrict** is now set to `1`:

- Runtime change applied with `sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1`.
- Persistent configuration updated in `/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf` (line now reads `kernel.kptr_restrict = 1`).

The system now complies with the **sysctl_kernel_kptr_restrict** security rule.

review_feedback: Review+QA approved but the verification scan failed. Try a different approach.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly sets kernel.kptr_restrict to the most restrictive value (2), ensures the setting is applied at runtime and persisted across reboots, and avoids duplicate/conflicting entries. The approach follows standard sysctl management practices and does not introduce service disruption.
concerns: Potential conflict with external configuration management tools that may overwrite the created file.
Setting the parameter to 2 may hinder debugging tools that rely on kernel address exposure.
suggested_improvements: Make the script idempotent by checking if the dedicated config file already contains the desired setting before overwriting.
Add handling for cases where the sysctl directories contain no *.conf files to avoid grep/sed errors.
Document or integrate the change into the organization’s configuration management system to prevent accidental revert.
